---
title: "Untitled"
format: html
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import requests
import re
import nltk
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer
from collections import Counter
from vaderSentiment.vaderSentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer
from textblob import TextBlob
import lda
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
```

```{python}
# Set plot style
sns.set_style("whitegrid")
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (12, 6)

data_path = "C:/Users/USER/Desktop/Unstructured Final Project/" 

company_news = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}company_news.csv')
earnings_surprises = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}earnings_surprises.csv')
basic_financials = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}basic_financials.csv')
stock_prices = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}stock_prices.csv')
general_market_news = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}general_market_news.csv')

###############################################################################
#  1.2  INSPECT THE MAIN DATASET: COMPANY NEWS
###############################################################################

# This is our PRIMARY dataset — 40k+ news articles with headlines and summaries
# This is the text data we'll run sentiment analysis and topic modeling on

print("\n--- Company News: Shape ---")
print(company_news.shape)

print("\n--- Company News: Columns ---")
print(company_news.columns.tolist())

print("\n--- Company News: First 3 Rows ---")
print(company_news.head(3))

print("\n--- Company News: Info ---")
company_news.info()

print("\n--- Company News: Describe ---")
print(company_news.describe())

# Check for missing values
print("\n--- Missing Values ---")
print(company_news.isnull().sum())

###############################################################################
#  1.3  CLEAN AND PREPARE COMPANY NEWS
###############################################################################

# Convert datetime column
# The Finnhub API returns datetime as Unix timestamp
# If 'datetime' column exists, convert it; otherwise check for 'date'
if 'datetime' in company_news.columns:
    company_news['date'] = pd.to_datetime(company_news['datetime'], unit='s')
elif 'date' in company_news.columns:
    company_news['date'] = pd.to_datetime(company_news['date'])

# Extract useful time features
company_news['year_month'] = company_news['date'].dt.to_period('M')
company_news['month'] = company_news['date'].dt.month
company_news['year'] = company_news['date'].dt.year
company_news['day_of_week'] = company_news['date'].dt.day_name()

# Check what text columns we have
# Finnhub company news typically has: headline, summary, source, url
text_cols = [col for col in company_news.columns if col in ['headline', 'summary', 'title']]
print(f"\n--- Text columns available: {text_cols} ---")

# Check a sample headline and summary
if 'headline' in company_news.columns:
    print("\nSample headlines:")
    print(company_news['headline'].head(5).tolist())

if 'summary' in company_news.columns:
    print("\nSample summaries:")
    print(company_news['summary'].head(3).tolist())

###############################################################################
#  1.4  ARTICLE DISTRIBUTION BY COMPANY
###############################################################################

# How many articles per company?
# The 'related' column in Finnhub contains the ticker symbol

# Identify the ticker column (could be 'related', 'symbol', or 'ticker')
ticker_col = None
for col in ['symbol', 'related', 'ticker']:
    if col in company_news.columns:
        ticker_col = col
        break

if ticker_col:
    print(f"\n--- Articles per Company (column: {ticker_col}) ---")
    articles_per_company = company_news[ticker_col].value_counts()
    print(articles_per_company)
    
    # Plot: Articles per company
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
    articles_per_company.plot(kind='bar', color='steelblue', ax=ax)
    ax.set_title('Number of News Articles per Company', fontsize=14)
    ax.set_xlabel('Company Ticker')
    ax.set_ylabel('Number of Articles')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(f'{data_path}plot_articles_per_company.png', dpi=150)
    plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  1.5  ARTICLE DISTRIBUTION OVER TIME
###############################################################################

# How are articles distributed over the 12-month collection period?
articles_over_time = company_news.groupby('year_month').size()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
articles_over_time.plot(kind='bar', color='coral', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Number of Articles Over Time (Monthly)', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Number of Articles')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(f'{data_path}plot_articles_over_time.png', dpi=150)
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  1.6  SECTOR MAPPING
###############################################################################

# Map companies to sectors for later cross-sector analysis
sector_map = {
    'AAPL': 'Technology', 'MSFT': 'Technology', 'GOOGL': 'Technology',
    'META': 'Technology', 'NVDA': 'Technology',
    'JPM': 'Finance', 'GS': 'Finance', 'BAC': 'Finance',
    'JNJ': 'Healthcare', 'UNH': 'Healthcare',
    'AMZN': 'Consumer', 'WMT': 'Consumer', 'KO': 'Consumer',
    'CAT': 'Industrial', 'BA': 'Industrial'
}

company_names = {
    'AAPL': 'Apple', 'MSFT': 'Microsoft', 'GOOGL': 'Alphabet',
    'META': 'Meta', 'NVDA': 'NVIDIA',
    'JPM': 'JPMorgan', 'GS': 'Goldman Sachs', 'BAC': 'Bank of America',
    'JNJ': 'Johnson & Johnson', 'UNH': 'UnitedHealth',
    'AMZN': 'Amazon', 'WMT': 'Walmart', 'KO': 'Coca-Cola',
    'CAT': 'Caterpillar', 'BA': 'Boeing'
}

if ticker_col:
    company_news['sector'] = company_news[ticker_col].map(sector_map)
    company_news['company_name'] = company_news[ticker_col].map(company_names)
    
    print("\n--- Articles per Sector ---")
    print(company_news['sector'].value_counts())
    
    # Plot: Articles by sector
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
    company_news['sector'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar', color='teal', ax=ax)
    ax.set_title('Articles by Sector', fontsize=14)
    ax.set_xlabel('Sector')
    ax.set_ylabel('Number of Articles')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(f'{data_path}plot_articles_by_sector.png', dpi=150)
    plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  1.7  TEXT LENGTH ANALYSIS
###############################################################################

# Understanding text length helps us decide preprocessing strategies

if 'headline' in company_news.columns:
    company_news['headline_word_count'] = company_news['headline'].astype(str).apply(
        lambda x: len(x.split())
    )
    print("\n--- Headline Word Count Stats ---")
    print(company_news['headline_word_count'].describe())

if 'summary' in company_news.columns:
    company_news['summary_word_count'] = company_news['summary'].astype(str).apply(
        lambda x: len(x.split())
    )
    print("\n--- Summary Word Count Stats ---")
    print(company_news['summary_word_count'].describe())
    
    # Plot: Distribution of summary lengths
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
    
    axes[0].hist(company_news['headline_word_count'], bins=30, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
    axes[0].set_title('Headline Word Count Distribution')
    axes[0].set_xlabel('Word Count')
    axes[0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    axes[1].hist(company_news['summary_word_count'], bins=30, color='coral', edgecolor='black')
    axes[1].set_title('Summary Word Count Distribution')
    axes[1].set_xlabel('Word Count')
    axes[1].set_ylabel('Frequency')
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(f'{data_path}plot_text_length_distribution.png', dpi=150)
    plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  1.8  INSPECT EARNINGS SURPRISES
###############################################################################

# This is our "outcome" data — did companies beat or miss estimates?

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  EARNINGS SURPRISES")
print("=" * 60)

print(earnings_surprises.head(10))
print(f"\nShape: {earnings_surprises.shape}")
print(f"\nColumns: {earnings_surprises.columns.tolist()}")

# If we have actual and estimate columns, calculate surprise percentage
if 'actual' in earnings_surprises.columns and 'estimate' in earnings_surprises.columns:
    # Surprise % might already be calculated; if not, compute it
    if 'surprise_pct' not in earnings_surprises.columns:
        earnings_surprises['surprise_pct'] = (
            (earnings_surprises['actual'] - earnings_surprises['estimate']) 
            / earnings_surprises['estimate'].abs() * 100
        )
    
    print("\n--- Earnings Surprise % Summary ---")
    print(earnings_surprises['surprise_pct'].describe())
    
    # Add sector mapping
    if 'symbol' in earnings_surprises.columns:
        earnings_surprises['sector'] = earnings_surprises['symbol'].map(sector_map)
    
    # Plot: Earnings surprises by company
    if 'symbol' in earnings_surprises.columns:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
        surprise_by_company = earnings_surprises.groupby('symbol')['surprise_pct'].mean()
        surprise_by_company.plot(kind='bar', color='green', ax=ax)
        ax.set_title('Average Earnings Surprise % by Company', fontsize=14)
        ax.set_xlabel('Company')
        ax.set_ylabel('Surprise %')
        ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
        plt.xticks(rotation=45)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(f'{data_path}plot_earnings_surprises.png', dpi=150)
        plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  1.9  INSPECT STOCK PRICES
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  STOCK PRICES")
print("=" * 60)

print(stock_prices.head())
print(f"\nShape: {stock_prices.shape}")
print(f"\nColumns: {stock_prices.columns.tolist()}")

# Check which column has the ticker
price_ticker_col = None
for col in ['symbol', 'ticker', 'Symbol', 'Ticker']:
    if col in stock_prices.columns:
        price_ticker_col = col
        break

if price_ticker_col:
    print(f"\nCompanies in stock data: {stock_prices[price_ticker_col].unique()}")

# Convert date
if 'Date' in stock_prices.columns:
    stock_prices['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(stock_prices['Date'])
elif 'date' in stock_prices.columns:
    stock_prices['date'] = pd.to_datetime(stock_prices['date'])

###############################################################################
#  1.10  INSPECT BASIC FINANCIALS
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  BASIC FINANCIALS")
print("=" * 60)

print(basic_financials.head())
print(f"\nShape: {basic_financials.shape}")
print(f"\nColumns (first 20): {basic_financials.columns.tolist()[:20]}")

###############################################################################
#  1.11  NEWS SOURCE ANALYSIS
###############################################################################

# What news sources are represented?
if 'source' in company_news.columns:
    print("\n--- Top 15 News Sources ---")
    print(company_news['source'].value_counts().head(15))
    
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
    company_news['source'].value_counts().head(15).plot(kind='barh', color='purple', ax=ax)
    ax.set_title('Top 15 News Sources', fontsize=14)
    ax.set_xlabel('Number of Articles')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(f'{data_path}plot_top_news_sources.png', dpi=150)
    plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  1.12  SUMMARY
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  PART 1 SUMMARY")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"""
  Company News:      {company_news.shape[0]:,} articles across {company_news[ticker_col].nunique() if ticker_col else 'N/A'} companies
  Earnings Data:     {earnings_surprises.shape[0]} records
  Stock Prices:      {stock_prices.shape[0]:,} trading day records
  Basic Financials:  {basic_financials.shape[0]} company snapshots
  Market News:       {general_market_news.shape[0]} articles
  
  Date Range:        {company_news['date'].min().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')} to {company_news['date'].max().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}
  Sectors:           {company_news['sector'].nunique() if 'sector' in company_news.columns else 'N/A'}
  
  NEXT STEP → Part 2: Text Preprocessing
""")

###############################################################################
#  SAVE THE CLEANED DATA FOR PART 2
###############################################################################

company_news.to_csv(f'{data_path}company_news_cleaned.csv', index=False)
print("Saved: company_news_cleaned.csv")

```

Text Preprocessing 

```{python}

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

# nltk.download('wordnet')
# nltk.download('omw-1.4')

###############################################################################
#  2.1  LOAD THE DATA FROM PART 1
###############################################################################

data_path = "C:/Users/USER/Desktop/Unstructured Final Project/"  # UPDATE to match your path

company_news = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}company_news_cleaned.csv')

print(f"Loaded {company_news.shape[0]:,} articles")
print(f"Columns: {company_news.columns.tolist()}")

###############################################################################
#  2.2  HANDLE MISSING TEXT
###############################################################################

print("\n--- Missing Values in Text Columns ---")
print(f"headline nulls:  {company_news['headline'].isnull().sum()}")
print(f"summary nulls:   {company_news['summary'].isnull().sum()}")

company_news['headline'] = company_news['headline'].astype(str)
company_news['summary'] = company_news['summary'].astype(str)

empty_summaries = (company_news['summary'].str.strip() == '').sum()
nan_string_summaries = (company_news['summary'] == 'nan').sum()
print(f"Empty summary strings:  {empty_summaries}")
print(f"'nan' summary strings:  {nan_string_summaries}")

company_news['summary'] = company_news['summary'].replace('nan', '')

# For articles with empty summaries, use the headline
mask = company_news['summary'].str.strip() == ''
company_news.loc[mask, 'summary'] = company_news.loc[mask, 'headline']
print(f"Filled {mask.sum()} empty summaries with headline text")

###############################################################################
#  2.3  TEXT NORMALIZATION (Lowercase)
###############################################################################

company_news['headline_clean'] = company_news['headline'].str.lower()
company_news['summary_clean'] = company_news['summary'].str.lower()

print("\n--- Sample After Lowercasing ---")
print(company_news['headline_clean'].head(3).tolist())

###############################################################################
#  2.4  REGEX CLEANING (Symbol and Word Removal)
###############################################################################

def clean_text(text):
    """
    Clean text using regex — same approach as class.
    Removes URLs, HTML tags, special chars, numbers, extra spaces.
    """
    text = re.sub(r'https?://\S+|www\.\S+', '', text)       # URLs
    text = re.sub(r'<.*?>', '', text)                         # HTML tags
    text = re.sub(r'\S+@\S+', '', text)                       # emails
    text = re.sub(r'\$([A-Za-z]+)', r'\1', text)              # $AAPL → AAPL
    text = re.sub(r'\([^)]*\)', '', text)                     # (NYSE: AAPL)
    text = re.sub(r"[().,?!;:'\"\-\[\]{}@#$%^&*_+=<>/\\|~`]", '', text)  # punctuation
    text = re.sub(r'\b\d+\b', '', text)                       # standalone numbers
    text = re.sub(r' +', ' ', text)                           # extra whitespace
    return text.strip()

company_news['headline_clean'] = company_news['headline_clean'].apply(clean_text)
company_news['summary_clean'] = company_news['summary_clean'].apply(clean_text)

print("\n--- Sample After Regex Cleaning ---")
print("BEFORE:", company_news['headline'].iloc[0])
print("AFTER: ", company_news['headline_clean'].iloc[0])

###############################################################################
#  2.5  STOPWORD REMOVAL
###############################################################################

# Same approach as class: download stopwords-iso list, build regex pattern

stopwords = requests.get(
    'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stopwords-iso/stopwords-en/refs/heads/master/stopwords-en.txt'
)
stopwords = stopwords.text.split('\n')

# Add domain-specific stopwords
finance_stopwords = [
    'said', 'says', 'also', 'would', 'could', 'one', 'new',
    'may', 'like', 'get', 'us', 'year', 'years', 'reuters',
    'yahoo', 'benzinga', 'seekingalpha', 'cnbc', 'marketwatch',
    'ap', 'bloomberg', 'inc', 'corp', 'ltd', 'llc', 'company',
    'stock', 'stocks', 'share', 'shares', 'market', 'markets',
    'price', 'percent', 'million', 'billion', 'quarter', 'analyst',
    'analysts', 'report', 'reported', 'reports', 'today', 'week'
]
[stopwords.append(x) for x in finance_stopwords]

stopword_pattern = r'\b(?:{})\b'.format('|'.join(stopwords))

company_news['headline_clean'] = company_news['headline_clean'].str.replace(
    stopword_pattern, '', regex=True
)
company_news['summary_clean'] = company_news['summary_clean'].str.replace(
    stopword_pattern, '', regex=True
)

# Clean up extra spaces after stopword removal
company_news['headline_clean'] = company_news['headline_clean'].str.replace(
    r' +', ' ', regex=True
).str.strip()
company_news['summary_clean'] = company_news['summary_clean'].str.replace(
    r' +', ' ', regex=True
).str.strip()

print("\n--- Sample After Stopword Removal ---")
print("BEFORE:", company_news['headline'].iloc[0].lower())
print("AFTER: ", company_news['headline_clean'].iloc[0])

###############################################################################
#  2.6  LEMMATIZATION
###############################################################################

# From class: NLTK WordNetLemmatizer (text_prep.qmd approach)

w_tokenizer = nltk.tokenize.WhitespaceTokenizer()
lemmatizer = nltk.stem.WordNetLemmatizer()

def lemmatize_text(text):
    """Lemmatize text — same approach as class."""
    return ' '.join([lemmatizer.lemmatize(w) for w in w_tokenizer.tokenize(text)])

print("\nLemmatizing headlines...")
company_news['headline_clean'] = company_news['headline_clean'].apply(lemmatize_text)

print("Lemmatizing summaries...")
company_news['summary_clean'] = company_news['summary_clean'].apply(lemmatize_text)

print("\n--- Sample After Lemmatization ---")
print(company_news['headline_clean'].head(5).tolist())

###############################################################################
#  2.7  WORD COUNT & FILTERING
###############################################################################

company_news['headline_wc_clean'] = company_news['headline_clean'].apply(
    lambda x: len(x.split())
)
company_news['summary_wc_clean'] = company_news['summary_clean'].apply(
    lambda x: len(x.split())
)

print("\n--- Clean Headline Word Count ---")
print(company_news['headline_wc_clean'].describe())

print("\n--- Clean Summary Word Count ---")
print(company_news['summary_wc_clean'].describe())

# Filter out articles with very short cleaned text
before = len(company_news)
company_news = company_news[company_news['summary_wc_clean'] >= 3]
print(f"\nRemoved {before - len(company_news)} articles with <3 clean summary words")
print(f"Remaining: {len(company_news):,} articles")

###############################################################################
#  2.8  COMBINE TEXT FOR ANALYSIS
###############################################################################

# headline alone is too short for LDA; combined gives better topics
company_news['combined_text'] = (
    company_news['headline_clean'] + ' ' + company_news['summary_clean']
)

print("\n--- Sample Combined Text ---")
print(company_news['combined_text'].iloc[0])

###############################################################################
#  2.9  TF-IDF ANALYSIS (by Sector)
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TF-IDF: DISTINGUISHING TERMS BY SECTOR")
print("=" * 60)

sector_texts = company_news.groupby('sector')['combined_text'].apply(
    lambda x: ' '.join(x)
).reset_index()

tfidf_vec = TfidfVectorizer(max_features=1000, min_df=2)
sector_tfidf = tfidf_vec.fit_transform(sector_texts['combined_text'])
tfidf_tokens = tfidf_vec.get_feature_names_out()

df_tfidf = pd.DataFrame(
    data=sector_tfidf.toarray(),
    index=sector_texts['sector'],
    columns=tfidf_tokens
)

print("\nTop 10 TF-IDF terms per sector:")
print("-" * 40)
for sector in df_tfidf.index:
    top_terms = df_tfidf.loc[sector].nlargest(10)
    print(f"\n{sector}:")
    for term, score in top_terms.items():
        print(f"  {term:20s} {score:.4f}")

###############################################################################
#  2.10  TF-IDF ANALYSIS (by Company)
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TF-IDF: DISTINGUISHING TERMS BY COMPANY")
print("=" * 60)

company_texts = company_news.groupby('symbol')['combined_text'].apply(
    lambda x: ' '.join(x)
).reset_index()

tfidf_vec_company = TfidfVectorizer(max_features=2000, min_df=2)
company_tfidf = tfidf_vec_company.fit_transform(company_texts['combined_text'])
tfidf_tokens_company = tfidf_vec_company.get_feature_names_out()

df_tfidf_company = pd.DataFrame(
    data=company_tfidf.toarray(),
    index=company_texts['symbol'],
    columns=tfidf_tokens_company
)

for ticker in ['AAPL', 'JPM', 'BA', 'NVDA', 'KO']:
    top_terms = df_tfidf_company.loc[ticker].nlargest(5)
    print(f"\n{ticker}:")
    for term, score in top_terms.items():
        print(f"  {term:20s} {score:.4f}")

###############################################################################
#  2.11  VISUALIZE: WORD FREQUENCY
###############################################################################

all_words = ' '.join(company_news['combined_text']).split()
word_freq = Counter(all_words)

top_25 = pd.DataFrame(word_freq.most_common(25), columns=['word', 'count'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
ax.barh(top_25['word'][::-1], top_25['count'][::-1], color='steelblue')
ax.set_title('Top 25 Most Frequent Words (After Cleaning)', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Frequency')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  PART 2 COMPLETE — company_news dataframe stays in memory for Parts 3-6
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  PART 2 COMPLETE")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"""
  Articles preprocessed:  {company_news.shape[0]:,}
  
  New columns added to company_news:
    - headline_clean           (lowercased, cleaned, stopwords removed, lemmatized)
    - summary_clean            (lowercased, cleaned, stopwords removed, lemmatized)
    - combined_text            (headline_clean + summary_clean)
    - headline_wc_clean
    - summary_wc_clean
  
  TF-IDF analysis complete (by sector and company)
  
  NEXT STEP → Part 3: Sentiment Analysis (VADER + TextBlob)
""")
```

Sentimental Analysis

```{python}
###############################################################################
#  PART 3: SENTIMENT ANALYSIS
###############################################################################
#
#  From class (sentiment_analysis.qmd):
#    - TextBlob: polarity and subjectivity
#    - VADER: compound scores (handles amplifiers, negators, adversatives)
#
#  IMPORTANT: We run sentiment on the ORIGINAL text (not cleaned).
#  VADER needs punctuation, capitalization, and words like "but" / "really"
#  to score properly. Remember from class:
#    "I hate code, but I love money" → VADER uses the adversative "but"
#    to downweight "hate" and amplify "love"
#
#  Continues from Part 2. company_news dataframe is already in memory.
#
###############################################################################

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

###############################################################################
#  3.1  VADER SENTIMENT ON HEADLINES
###############################################################################

# VADER works best on short text (headlines are avg ~12 words — perfect)
# We use the ORIGINAL headline, not the cleaned version

vader = SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()

print("Running VADER on headlines...")

# Apply VADER to each headline — returns compound, pos, neg, neu scores
company_news['vader_compound'] = company_news['headline'].apply(
    lambda x: vader.polarity_scores(str(x)).get('compound')
)
company_news['vader_pos'] = company_news['headline'].apply(
    lambda x: vader.polarity_scores(str(x)).get('pos')
)
company_news['vader_neg'] = company_news['headline'].apply(
    lambda x: vader.polarity_scores(str(x)).get('neg')
)
company_news['vader_neu'] = company_news['headline'].apply(
    lambda x: vader.polarity_scores(str(x)).get('neu')
)

print("VADER complete.")

print("\n--- VADER Compound Score Summary ---")
print(company_news['vader_compound'].describe())

# Classify sentiment into categories
company_news['vader_label'] = company_news['vader_compound'].apply(
    lambda x: 'positive' if x >= 0.05 else ('negative' if x <= -0.05 else 'neutral')
)

print("\n--- VADER Sentiment Distribution ---")
print(company_news['vader_label'].value_counts())
print(company_news['vader_label'].value_counts(normalize=True).round(3))

###############################################################################
#  3.2  TEXTBLOB SENTIMENT ON HEADLINES
###############################################################################

# TextBlob gives us polarity (-1 to 1) and subjectivity (0 to 1)
# From class: blob.polarity and blob.subjectivity

print("\nRunning TextBlob on headlines...")

company_news['tb_polarity'] = company_news['headline'].apply(
    lambda x: TextBlob(str(x)).polarity
)
company_news['tb_subjectivity'] = company_news['headline'].apply(
    lambda x: TextBlob(str(x)).subjectivity
)

print("TextBlob complete.")

print("\n--- TextBlob Polarity Summary ---")
print(company_news['tb_polarity'].describe())

print("\n--- TextBlob Subjectivity Summary ---")
print(company_news['tb_subjectivity'].describe())

###############################################################################
#  3.3  COMPARE VADER vs TEXTBLOB
###############################################################################

# From class we learned these tools can disagree
# Let's see how correlated they are

correlation = company_news['vader_compound'].corr(company_news['tb_polarity'])
print(f"\n--- VADER vs TextBlob Correlation: {correlation:.4f} ---")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(
    company_news['vader_compound'].sample(2000, random_state=42),
    company_news['tb_polarity'].sample(2000, random_state=42),
    alpha=0.3, s=10, color='steelblue'
)
ax.set_xlabel('VADER Compound')
ax.set_ylabel('TextBlob Polarity')
ax.set_title(f'VADER vs TextBlob Sentiment (r = {correlation:.3f})')
ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
ax.axvline(x=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  3.4  SENTIMENT BY COMPANY
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  SENTIMENT BY COMPANY")
print("=" * 60)

sentiment_by_company = company_news.groupby('symbol').agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean'),
    vader_std=('vader_compound', 'std'),
    tb_mean=('tb_polarity', 'mean'),
    article_count=('vader_compound', 'count')
).round(4)

print(sentiment_by_company.sort_values('vader_mean', ascending=False))

# Plot: Average VADER sentiment by company
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
sentiment_sorted = sentiment_by_company.sort_values('vader_mean')
colors = ['red' if x < 0 else 'green' for x in sentiment_sorted['vader_mean']]
ax.barh(sentiment_sorted.index, sentiment_sorted['vader_mean'], color=colors)
ax.set_xlabel('Average VADER Compound Score')
ax.set_title('Average Headline Sentiment by Company (VADER)', fontsize=14)
ax.axvline(x=0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  3.5  SENTIMENT BY SECTOR
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  SENTIMENT BY SECTOR")
print("=" * 60)

sentiment_by_sector = company_news.groupby('sector').agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean'),
    vader_std=('vader_compound', 'std'),
    tb_mean=('tb_polarity', 'mean'),
    tb_subj_mean=('tb_subjectivity', 'mean'),
    article_count=('vader_compound', 'count')
).round(4)

print(sentiment_by_sector.sort_values('vader_mean', ascending=False))

# Plot: Sentiment distribution by sector (box plot)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
company_news.boxplot(column='vader_compound', by='sector', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('VADER Sentiment Distribution by Sector', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Sector')
ax.set_ylabel('VADER Compound Score')
plt.suptitle('')  # remove default pandas title
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  3.6  SENTIMENT OVER TIME
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  SENTIMENT OVER TIME")
print("=" * 60)

# Convert date if needed
company_news['date'] = pd.to_datetime(company_news['date'])
company_news['year_month'] = company_news['date'].dt.to_period('M')

# Monthly average sentiment
monthly_sentiment = company_news.groupby('year_month').agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean'),
    article_count=('vader_compound', 'count')
).reset_index()

monthly_sentiment['year_month_str'] = monthly_sentiment['year_month'].astype(str)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 6))
ax.plot(
    monthly_sentiment['year_month_str'],
    monthly_sentiment['vader_mean'],
    marker='o', color='steelblue', linewidth=2
)
ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Average VADER Compound')
ax.set_title('Average Headline Sentiment Over Time', fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  3.7  SENTIMENT OVER TIME BY SECTOR
###############################################################################

monthly_sector = company_news.groupby(['year_month', 'sector']).agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean')
).reset_index()

monthly_sector['year_month_str'] = monthly_sector['year_month'].astype(str)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 6))
for sector in monthly_sector['sector'].unique():
    subset = monthly_sector[monthly_sector['sector'] == sector]
    ax.plot(
        subset['year_month_str'],
        subset['vader_mean'],
        marker='o', label=sector, linewidth=1.5
    )
ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Average VADER Compound')
ax.set_title('Sentiment Over Time by Sector', fontsize=14)
ax.legend(loc='best')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  3.8  MOST POSITIVE AND NEGATIVE HEADLINES
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  MOST POSITIVE HEADLINES")
print("=" * 60)

top_positive = company_news.nlargest(10, 'vader_compound')[
    ['symbol', 'headline', 'vader_compound']
]
for _, row in top_positive.iterrows():
    print(f"  [{row['symbol']}] ({row['vader_compound']:.3f}) {row['headline'][:80]}")

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  MOST NEGATIVE HEADLINES")
print("=" * 60)

top_negative = company_news.nsmallest(10, 'vader_compound')[
    ['symbol', 'headline', 'vader_compound']
]
for _, row in top_negative.iterrows():
    print(f"  [{row['symbol']}] ({row['vader_compound']:.3f}) {row['headline'][:80]}")

###############################################################################
#  3.9  SENTIMENT DISTRIBUTION VISUALIZATION
###############################################################################

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# VADER distribution
axes[0].hist(company_news['vader_compound'], bins=50, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
axes[0].set_title('VADER Compound Score Distribution')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Compound Score')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[0].axvline(x=0, color='red', linestyle='--')

# TextBlob distribution
axes[1].hist(company_news['tb_polarity'], bins=50, color='coral', edgecolor='black')
axes[1].set_title('TextBlob Polarity Distribution')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Polarity')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[1].axvline(x=0, color='red', linestyle='--')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  3.10  POSITIVE vs NEGATIVE ARTICLE RATIO BY COMPANY
###############################################################################

sentiment_ratio = company_news.groupby('symbol')['vader_label'].value_counts(
    normalize=True
).unstack(fill_value=0).round(3)

print("\n--- Sentiment Ratio by Company ---")
print(sentiment_ratio)

# Stacked bar chart
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
sentiment_ratio[['positive', 'neutral', 'negative']].plot(
    kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax,
    color=['green', 'gray', 'red'], alpha=0.8
)
ax.set_title('Sentiment Distribution by Company', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Company')
ax.set_ylabel('Proportion')
ax.legend(title='Sentiment')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  PART 3 COMPLETE — company_news now has sentiment columns for Parts 4-6
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  PART 3 COMPLETE")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"""
  New columns added to company_news:
    - vader_compound      (VADER compound score, -1 to 1)
    - vader_pos           (VADER positive proportion)
    - vader_neg           (VADER negative proportion)
    - vader_neu           (VADER neutral proportion)
    - vader_label         (positive / neutral / negative)
    - tb_polarity         (TextBlob polarity, -1 to 1)
    - tb_subjectivity     (TextBlob subjectivity, 0 to 1)
  
  NEXT STEP → Part 4: Topic Modeling (LDA)
""")
```

Topic Modeling

```{python}
###############################################################################
#  PART 4: TOPIC MODELING (LDA)
###############################################################################
#
#  From class (topic_model_demo.py):
#    - CountVectorizer → document-term matrix
#    - lda.LDA → fit topic model
#    - Extract top words per topic
#    - Assign top_topic and top_prob to each document
#    - Track topic evolution over time
#    - OLS regression on topic probabilities (same as class demo)
#
#  We use the CLEANED combined_text from Part 2.
#  company_news dataframe is already in memory from Parts 2-3.
#
###############################################################################

###############################################################################
#  4.1  BUILD DOCUMENT-TERM MATRIX
###############################################################################

# Same as class: CountVectorizer → fit_transform on cleaned text

vectorizer = CountVectorizer()

X = vectorizer.fit_transform(company_news['combined_text'])

vocab = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

print(f"Document-Term Matrix shape: {X.shape}")
print(f"  Documents: {X.shape[0]:,}")
print(f"  Vocabulary size: {X.shape[1]:,}")

###############################################################################
#  4.2  FIT LDA MODEL
###############################################################################

# Same as class demo: lda.LDA with n_topics and n_iter
# Starting with 5 topics (same as class demo)
# n_iter=500 matches class; increase if needed

model = lda.LDA(n_topics=5, n_iter=500, random_state=1)

print("\nFitting LDA model (5 topics, 500 iterations)...")
print("This may take a few minutes with 39k documents...")

model.fit(X)

print("LDA model fit complete.")

###############################################################################
#  4.3  EXTRACT TOP WORDS PER TOPIC
###############################################################################

# Same as class: topic_word matrix → argsort → top N words

topic_word = model.topic_word_

n_top_words = 10

top_words = []

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TOP WORDS PER TOPIC")
print("=" * 60)

for i, topic_dist in enumerate(topic_word):
    topic_words = np.array(vocab)[np.argsort(topic_dist)][:-n_top_words:-1]
    top_words.append(' '.join(topic_words))
    print(f'\nTopic {i}: {" ".join(topic_words)}')

###############################################################################
#  4.4  ASSIGN TOPICS TO DOCUMENTS
###############################################################################

# Same as class: argmax for top topic, max for top probability

company_news['top_topic'] = np.argmax(model.doc_topic_, axis=1)

company_news['top_prob'] = [
    max(model.doc_topic_[x]) for x in range(len(model.doc_topic_))
]

print("\n--- Topic Distribution ---")
print(company_news['top_topic'].value_counts().sort_index())

print("\n--- Top Probability Summary ---")
print(company_news['top_prob'].describe())

###############################################################################
#  4.5  ADD INDIVIDUAL TOPIC PROBABILITIES
###############################################################################

# Same as class: assign each topic's probability as its own column

for i in range(5):
    company_news[f'topic_{i+1}'] = model.doc_topic_[:, i]

###############################################################################
#  4.6  TOPIC DISTRIBUTION BY SECTOR
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TOPICS BY SECTOR")
print("=" * 60)

topic_by_sector = company_news.groupby('sector')['top_topic'].value_counts(
    normalize=True
).unstack(fill_value=0).round(3)

print(topic_by_sector)

# Heatmap: Topic distribution across sectors
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.heatmap(
    topic_by_sector, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='YlOrRd',
    xticklabels=[f'Topic {i}\n{top_words[i][:30]}...' for i in range(5)],
    ax=ax
)
ax.set_title('Topic Distribution by Sector', fontsize=14)
ax.set_ylabel('Sector')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  4.7  TOPIC DISTRIBUTION BY COMPANY
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TOPICS BY COMPANY")
print("=" * 60)

topic_by_company = company_news.groupby('symbol')['top_topic'].value_counts(
    normalize=True
).unstack(fill_value=0).round(3)

print(topic_by_company)

# Heatmap
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.heatmap(
    topic_by_company, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='YlOrRd',
    xticklabels=[f'Topic {i}' for i in range(5)],
    ax=ax
)
ax.set_title('Topic Distribution by Company', fontsize=14)
ax.set_ylabel('Company')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  4.8  TOPIC EVOLUTION OVER TIME
###############################################################################

# Same as class: aggregate by top_topic and time, plot

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TOPIC EVOLUTION OVER TIME")
print("=" * 60)

company_news['date'] = pd.to_datetime(company_news['date'])
company_news['year_month_dt'] = company_news['date'].dt.to_period('M')

# Filter to the main data window (2025-03 to 2026-02) to avoid sparse months
company_news_main = company_news[company_news['date'] >= '2025-03-01'].copy()

aggregated = company_news_main.groupby(
    ['top_topic', 'year_month_dt']
)['top_prob'].mean().to_frame(name='prob').reset_index()

aggregated['year_month_str'] = aggregated['year_month_dt'].astype(str)

# Same plot style as class: lineplot with hue
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 6))
sns.lineplot(
    x='year_month_str', y='prob', data=aggregated,
    hue='top_topic', marker='o', ax=ax
)
ax.set_title('Average Topic Probability Over Time', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Average Probability')
ax.legend(title='Topic', labels=[f'T{i}: {top_words[i][:25]}...' for i in range(5)])
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  4.9  OLS REGRESSION ON TOPICS OVER TIME
###############################################################################

# Same as class demo: smf.ols("topic_X ~ year", data)
# We use a numeric month index instead of year since our data spans ~12 months

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  OLS: TOPIC PROBABILITIES OVER TIME")
print("=" * 60)

# Create a numeric time variable (months since start)
company_news_main['month_num'] = (
    company_news_main['date'].dt.year * 12 + company_news_main['date'].dt.month
)
min_month = company_news_main['month_num'].min()
company_news_main['month_index'] = company_news_main['month_num'] - min_month

# Run OLS for each topic (same pattern as class demo)
ols_results = {}
predictions = {}

for i in range(5):
    formula = f"topic_{i+1} ~ month_index"
    mf = smf.ols(formula, company_news_main)
    mdf = mf.fit()
    ols_results[i] = mdf
    predictions[i] = mdf.get_prediction()

    print(f"\n--- Topic {i+1} ---")
    print(f"  Coefficient (month_index): {mdf.params['month_index']:.6f}")
    print(f"  P-value:                   {mdf.pvalues['month_index']:.4f}")
    print(f"  R-squared:                 {mdf.rsquared:.4f}")
    direction = "↑ increasing" if mdf.params['month_index'] > 0 else "↓ decreasing"
    sig = "***" if mdf.pvalues['month_index'] < 0.001 else (
        "**" if mdf.pvalues['month_index'] < 0.01 else (
            "*" if mdf.pvalues['month_index'] < 0.05 else "ns"
        )
    )
    print(f"  Trend: {direction} ({sig})")

# Combined prediction plot (same as class demo)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

for i in range(5):
    ax.plot(
        company_news_main['month_index'],
        predictions[i].predicted_mean,
        label=f"T{i+1}: {top_words[i][:20]}..."
    )

ax.set_xlabel('Month Index')
ax.set_ylabel('Predicted Topic Probability')
ax.set_title('OLS: Topic Probability Trends Over Time', fontsize=14)
legend = ax.legend(loc="best")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  4.10  MIXED EFFECTS MODEL
###############################################################################

# Same as class: mixed linear model with topic as group

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  MIXED EFFECTS MODEL")
print("=" * 60)

mf_mixed = smf.mixedlm(
    "top_prob ~ month_index",
    company_news_main,
    groups=company_news_main["top_topic"]
)

mdf_mixed = mf_mixed.fit()

print(mdf_mixed.summary())

###############################################################################
#  4.11  SAMPLE ARTICLES PER TOPIC
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  SAMPLE HEADLINES PER TOPIC")
print("=" * 60)

for topic_id in range(5):
    topic_articles = company_news[company_news['top_topic'] == topic_id].nlargest(
        5, 'top_prob'
    )
    print(f"\n--- Topic {topic_id}: {top_words[topic_id][:40]} ---")
    for _, row in topic_articles.iterrows():
        print(f"  [{row['symbol']}] (prob={row['top_prob']:.3f}) {row['headline'][:75]}")

###############################################################################
#  4.12  TOPIC-SENTIMENT RELATIONSHIP
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  TOPIC vs SENTIMENT")
print("=" * 60)

topic_sentiment = company_news.groupby('top_topic').agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean'),
    vader_std=('vader_compound', 'std'),
    count=('vader_compound', 'count')
).round(4)

for i in range(5):
    topic_sentiment.loc[i, 'top_words'] = top_words[i][:40]

print(topic_sentiment)

# Box plot: sentiment by topic
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
company_news.boxplot(column='vader_compound', by='top_topic', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('VADER Sentiment by Topic', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Topic')
ax.set_ylabel('VADER Compound Score')
plt.suptitle('')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  PART 4 COMPLETE — company_news now has topic columns for Parts 5-6
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  PART 4 COMPLETE")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"""
  New columns added to company_news:
    - top_topic           (0-4, most likely topic)
    - top_prob            (probability of top topic)
    - topic_1 ... topic_5 (probability of each topic)
  
  LDA model with 5 topics fitted on {len(company_news):,} articles
  OLS regression and mixed effects model complete
  
  NEXT STEP → Part 5: Market Impact Analysis
               (correlate sentiment + topics with stock prices & earnings)
""")
```

Market Impact Analysis

```{python}
###############################################################################
#  PART 5: MARKET IMPACT ANALYSIS
###############################################################################
#
#  This is where we bring it all together:
#    - Does news sentiment predict stock price movements?
#    - Do topic distributions relate to earnings surprises?
#    - OLS regression: sentiment → returns, topics → returns
#
#  Uses company_news (with sentiment + topic columns) from Parts 2-4,
#  plus earnings_surprises and stock_prices from Part 1.
#
###############################################################################

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

sns.set_style("whitegrid")

###############################################################################
#  5.1  LOAD STOCK PRICES & EARNINGS (from Part 1)
###############################################################################

data_path = ""  # UPDATE to match your path

# Load earnings (this one is clean from Part 1)
earnings_surprises = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}earnings_surprises.csv')

# Load stock prices — this has a multi-header format from yfinance
# Row 0 = metric names (Close, High, Low, Open, Volume)
# Row 1 = ticker symbols
# Row 2+ = actual data with dates in first column
stock_raw = pd.read_csv(f'{data_path}stock_prices.csv', header=[0, 1])

print("Raw stock data shape:", stock_raw.shape)
print("First few column pairs:", list(stock_raw.columns[:6]))

###############################################################################
#  5.2  CLEAN STOCK PRICES
###############################################################################

# The yfinance multi-index header needs to be reshaped
# We want: date | symbol | close | high | low | open | volume

# Flatten the multi-index columns
stock_raw.columns = ['_'.join(col).strip() for col in stock_raw.columns]

print("\nFlattened columns (first 10):", stock_raw.columns[:10].tolist())

# The first column should be the date (Price_Ticker or similar)
# Let's identify it
date_col = stock_raw.columns[0]
print(f"Date column: {date_col}")

# Skip the row that has 'date' or 'Ticker' as values (row 0 after header)
stock_raw = stock_raw[stock_raw[date_col] != 'date']
stock_raw = stock_raw[stock_raw[date_col] != 'Ticker']

# Identify Close columns for each ticker
close_cols = [col for col in stock_raw.columns if col.startswith('Close')]
tickers_in_data = [col.replace('Close_', '') for col in close_cols]

print(f"\nTickers found: {tickers_in_data}")

# Build a clean long-format dataframe
stock_records = []

for ticker in tickers_in_data:
    close_col = f'Close_{ticker}'
    if close_col in stock_raw.columns:
        temp = stock_raw[[date_col, close_col]].copy()
        temp.columns = ['date', 'close']
        temp['symbol'] = ticker
        temp['close'] = pd.to_numeric(temp['close'], errors='coerce')
        temp['date'] = pd.to_datetime(temp['date'], errors='coerce')
        temp = temp.dropna(subset=['date', 'close'])
        stock_records.append(temp)

stock_prices = pd.concat(stock_records, ignore_index=True)
stock_prices = stock_prices.sort_values(['symbol', 'date']).reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"\nClean stock data: {stock_prices.shape[0]:,} rows")
print(stock_prices.head())

###############################################################################
#  5.3  CALCULATE STOCK RETURNS
###############################################################################

# Daily returns = (close_today - close_yesterday) / close_yesterday
stock_prices['daily_return'] = stock_prices.groupby('symbol')['close'].pct_change()

# Weekly returns (5-day rolling)
stock_prices['weekly_return'] = stock_prices.groupby('symbol')['close'].pct_change(5)

# Monthly returns
stock_prices['year_month'] = stock_prices['date'].dt.to_period('M')

monthly_returns = stock_prices.groupby(['symbol', 'year_month']).agg(
    monthly_return=('daily_return', lambda x: (1 + x).prod() - 1),
    avg_close=('close', 'mean'),
    volatility=('daily_return', 'std')
).reset_index()

print("\n--- Monthly Returns Sample ---")
print(monthly_returns.head(10))

###############################################################################
#  5.4  AGGREGATE MONTHLY SENTIMENT
###############################################################################

# Aggregate news sentiment by company and month
company_news['date'] = pd.to_datetime(company_news['date'])
company_news['year_month'] = company_news['date'].dt.to_period('M')

monthly_sentiment = company_news.groupby(['symbol', 'year_month']).agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean'),
    vader_std=('vader_compound', 'std'),
    tb_mean=('tb_polarity', 'mean'),
    pct_positive=('vader_label', lambda x: (x == 'positive').mean()),
    pct_negative=('vader_label', lambda x: (x == 'negative').mean()),
    article_count=('vader_compound', 'count'),
    top_topic_mode=('top_topic', lambda x: x.mode().iloc[0] if len(x.mode()) > 0 else np.nan),
    topic_1_mean=('topic_1', 'mean'),
    topic_2_mean=('topic_2', 'mean'),
    topic_3_mean=('topic_3', 'mean'),
    topic_4_mean=('topic_4', 'mean'),
    topic_5_mean=('topic_5', 'mean')
).reset_index()

print(f"\n--- Monthly Sentiment: {monthly_sentiment.shape[0]} rows ---")
print(monthly_sentiment.head())

###############################################################################
#  5.5  MERGE SENTIMENT WITH RETURNS
###############################################################################

# Merge on symbol + year_month
merged = monthly_returns.merge(
    monthly_sentiment,
    on=['symbol', 'year_month'],
    how='inner'
)

print(f"\n--- Merged Dataset: {merged.shape[0]} rows ---")
print(merged.columns.tolist())
print(merged.head())

###############################################################################
#  5.6  CORRELATION ANALYSIS
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  CORRELATION: SENTIMENT vs RETURNS")
print("=" * 60)

corr_cols = [
    'monthly_return', 'volatility', 'vader_mean', 'tb_mean',
    'pct_positive', 'pct_negative', 'article_count',
    'topic_1_mean', 'topic_2_mean', 'topic_3_mean',
    'topic_4_mean', 'topic_5_mean'
]

corr_matrix = merged[corr_cols].corr().round(3)

# Show correlations with monthly_return
print("\nCorrelation with Monthly Return:")
print(corr_matrix['monthly_return'].sort_values(ascending=False))

# Heatmap
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 10))
sns.heatmap(
    corr_matrix, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='RdBu_r',
    center=0, ax=ax, vmin=-1, vmax=1
)
ax.set_title('Correlation Matrix: Sentiment, Topics & Returns', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  5.7  SCATTER: SENTIMENT vs RETURNS
###############################################################################

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# VADER vs Returns
axes[0].scatter(merged['vader_mean'], merged['monthly_return'],
                alpha=0.5, s=30, color='steelblue')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Average VADER Sentiment')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Monthly Return')
axes[0].set_title('VADER Sentiment vs Monthly Return')
axes[0].axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
axes[0].axvline(x=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

# % Negative vs Returns
axes[1].scatter(merged['pct_negative'], merged['monthly_return'],
                alpha=0.5, s=30, color='coral')
axes[1].set_xlabel('% Negative Headlines')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Monthly Return')
axes[1].set_title('% Negative Headlines vs Monthly Return')
axes[1].axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  5.8  OLS: SENTIMENT → RETURNS
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  OLS: SENTIMENT PREDICTING RETURNS")
print("=" * 60)

# Model 1: VADER sentiment → returns
model1 = smf.ols("monthly_return ~ vader_mean", merged).fit()
print("\n--- Model 1: VADER → Returns ---")
print(f"  Coefficient: {model1.params['vader_mean']:.4f}")
print(f"  P-value:     {model1.pvalues['vader_mean']:.4f}")
print(f"  R-squared:   {model1.rsquared:.4f}")

# Model 2: Multiple sentiment features → returns
model2 = smf.ols(
    "monthly_return ~ vader_mean + pct_negative + article_count + volatility",
    merged
).fit()
print("\n--- Model 2: Multiple Sentiment Features → Returns ---")
print(model2.summary().tables[1])

# Model 3: Topic probabilities → returns
model3 = smf.ols(
    "monthly_return ~ topic_1_mean + topic_2_mean + topic_3_mean + topic_4_mean + topic_5_mean",
    merged
).fit()
print("\n--- Model 3: Topic Probabilities → Returns ---")
print(model3.summary().tables[1])

# Model 4: Full model (sentiment + topics)
model4 = smf.ols(
    "monthly_return ~ vader_mean + pct_negative + topic_1_mean + topic_2_mean + topic_3_mean + topic_4_mean + topic_5_mean",
    merged
).fit()
print("\n--- Model 4: Full Model (Sentiment + Topics) → Returns ---")
print(model4.summary().tables[1])
print(f"  R-squared: {model4.rsquared:.4f}")

###############################################################################
#  5.9  EARNINGS SURPRISE ANALYSIS
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  EARNINGS SURPRISE vs SENTIMENT")
print("=" * 60)

# Add sector mapping to earnings
sector_map = {
    'AAPL': 'Technology', 'MSFT': 'Technology', 'GOOGL': 'Technology',
    'META': 'Technology', 'NVDA': 'Technology',
    'JPM': 'Finance', 'GS': 'Finance', 'BAC': 'Finance',
    'JNJ': 'Healthcare', 'UNH': 'Healthcare',
    'AMZN': 'Consumer', 'WMT': 'Consumer', 'KO': 'Consumer',
    'CAT': 'Industrial', 'BA': 'Industrial'
}

# Get the surprisePercent column (from your EDA output)
surprise_col = None
for col in ['surprisePercent', 'surprise_pct', 'surprisepercentage']:
    if col in earnings_surprises.columns:
        surprise_col = col
        break

if surprise_col is None and 'actual' in earnings_surprises.columns:
    earnings_surprises['surprise_pct'] = (
        (earnings_surprises['actual'] - earnings_surprises['estimate'])
        / earnings_surprises['estimate'].abs() * 100
    )
    surprise_col = 'surprise_pct'

# Average sentiment by company (overall)
company_sentiment = company_news.groupby('symbol').agg(
    vader_mean=('vader_compound', 'mean'),
    pct_negative=('vader_label', lambda x: (x == 'negative').mean()),
    pct_positive=('vader_label', lambda x: (x == 'positive').mean()),
    dominant_topic=('top_topic', lambda x: x.mode().iloc[0])
).reset_index()

# Average earnings surprise by company
company_earnings = earnings_surprises.groupby('symbol').agg(
    avg_surprise=(surprise_col, 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Merge
earnings_sentiment = company_sentiment.merge(company_earnings, on='symbol', how='inner')
earnings_sentiment['sector'] = earnings_sentiment['symbol'].map(sector_map)

print(earnings_sentiment[['symbol', 'sector', 'vader_mean', 'pct_negative',
                           'avg_surprise', 'dominant_topic']].to_string())

# Scatter: Sentiment vs Earnings Surprise
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
scatter = ax.scatter(
    earnings_sentiment['vader_mean'],
    earnings_sentiment['avg_surprise'],
    s=100, c=earnings_sentiment['dominant_topic'], cmap='Set1',
    edgecolors='black', linewidth=0.5
)
for _, row in earnings_sentiment.iterrows():
    ax.annotate(row['symbol'], (row['vader_mean'], row['avg_surprise']),
                fontsize=9, ha='left', va='bottom')
ax.set_xlabel('Average VADER Sentiment')
ax.set_ylabel('Average Earnings Surprise %')
ax.set_title('News Sentiment vs Earnings Surprise by Company', fontsize=14)
ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
plt.colorbar(scatter, label='Dominant Topic')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# OLS: Sentiment → Earnings Surprise
if len(earnings_sentiment) >= 10:
    es_model = smf.ols("avg_surprise ~ vader_mean + pct_negative", earnings_sentiment).fit()
    print("\n--- OLS: Sentiment → Earnings Surprise ---")
    print(es_model.summary().tables[1])
    print(f"  R-squared: {es_model.rsquared:.4f}")

###############################################################################
#  5.10  SENTIMENT AROUND EARNINGS DATES
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  SENTIMENT AROUND EARNINGS DATES")
print("=" * 60)

# Check if earnings has a period/date column
if 'period' in earnings_surprises.columns:
    earnings_surprises['earnings_date'] = pd.to_datetime(
        earnings_surprises['period'], errors='coerce'
    )

    # For each earnings event, get sentiment in the 30 days before
    pre_earnings_sentiment = []

    for _, earn_row in earnings_surprises.iterrows():
        if pd.isna(earn_row['earnings_date']):
            continue
        ticker = earn_row['symbol']
        earn_date = earn_row['earnings_date']
        window_start = earn_date - pd.Timedelta(days=30)

        window_news = company_news[
            (company_news['symbol'] == ticker) &
            (company_news['date'] >= window_start) &
            (company_news['date'] <= earn_date)
        ]

        if len(window_news) > 0:
            pre_earnings_sentiment.append({
                'symbol': ticker,
                'earnings_date': earn_date,
                'surprise': earn_row[surprise_col],
                'pre_vader_mean': window_news['vader_compound'].mean(),
                'pre_pct_negative': (window_news['vader_label'] == 'negative').mean(),
                'pre_article_count': len(window_news),
                'quarter': earn_row.get('quarter', np.nan)
            })

    if pre_earnings_sentiment:
        pre_earn_df = pd.DataFrame(pre_earnings_sentiment)
        print(pre_earn_df.to_string())

        # Correlation
        corr = pre_earn_df['pre_vader_mean'].corr(pre_earn_df['surprise'])
        print(f"\nCorrelation (pre-earnings sentiment vs surprise): {corr:.4f}")

        # Scatter
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
        ax.scatter(pre_earn_df['pre_vader_mean'], pre_earn_df['surprise'],
                   s=80, color='steelblue', edgecolors='black')
        for _, row in pre_earn_df.iterrows():
            ax.annotate(f"{row['symbol']}", (row['pre_vader_mean'], row['surprise']),
                        fontsize=8)
        ax.set_xlabel('Pre-Earnings VADER Sentiment (30-day avg)')
        ax.set_ylabel('Earnings Surprise %')
        ax.set_title('Pre-Earnings Sentiment vs Actual Surprise', fontsize=14)
        ax.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  PART 5 COMPLETE
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 60)
print("  PART 5 COMPLETE")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"""
  Analyses completed:
    - Stock returns calculated (daily, weekly, monthly)
    - Monthly sentiment aggregated and merged with returns
    - Correlation matrix: sentiment, topics, returns
    - OLS regressions:
        Model 1: VADER → returns
        Model 2: Multiple sentiment → returns
        Model 3: Topic probabilities → returns
        Model 4: Full model (sentiment + topics) → returns
    - Earnings surprise vs sentiment analysis
    - Pre-earnings sentiment window analysis
  
  NEXT STEP → Part 6: Final Summary & Conclusions
""")
```


Conclusion


```{python}
###############################################################################
#  PART 6: FINAL SUMMARY & CONCLUSIONS
###############################################################################
#
#  This part:
#    1. Summarizes all key findings from Parts 1-5
#    2. Creates polished visualizations for the final report
#    3. Prints a structured conclusion
#
#  Continues from Parts 2-5. All dataframes in memory.
#
###############################################################################

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

sns.set_style("whitegrid")
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (14, 7)
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 11

###############################################################################
#  6.1  EXECUTIVE DASHBOARD — 4-PANEL SUMMARY FIGURE
###############################################################################

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(18, 14))
gs = GridSpec(2, 2, figure=fig, hspace=0.35, wspace=0.3)

# --- Panel 1: Sentiment by Company ---
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 0])
sent_by_company = company_news.groupby('symbol')['vader_compound'].mean().sort_values()
colors = ['#d32f2f' if x < 0.09 else '#388e3c' if x > 0.12 else '#1976d2'
          for x in sent_by_company.values]
ax1.barh(sent_by_company.index, sent_by_company.values, color=colors)
ax1.set_xlabel('Average VADER Compound')
ax1.set_title('A. Average Headline Sentiment by Company', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax1.axvline(x=sent_by_company.mean(), color='black', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5,
            label=f'Mean: {sent_by_company.mean():.3f}')
ax1.legend(fontsize=9)

# --- Panel 2: Topic Distribution (Pie) ---
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1])
topic_counts = company_news['top_topic'].value_counts().sort_index()
topic_labels = [
    'T0: Corporate/Retail\n(johnson, walmart, amazon)',
    'T1: Political/Trade\n(trump, boeing, tariff)',
    'T2: Market/Trading\n(earnings, dow, wall st)',
    'T3: Banking/Investment\n(dividend, bank, fund)',
    'T4: Big Tech/AI\n(nvidia, meta, apple)'
]
topic_colors = ['#42a5f5', '#ef5350', '#66bb6a', '#ffa726', '#ab47bc']
wedges, texts, autotexts = ax2.pie(
    topic_counts.values, labels=topic_labels, autopct='%1.1f%%',
    colors=topic_colors, startangle=90, textprops={'fontsize': 9}
)
ax2.set_title('B. Topic Distribution Across All Articles', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

# --- Panel 3: Sentiment Over Time ---
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0])
company_news_main = company_news[company_news['date'] >= '2025-03-01'].copy()
monthly_sent = company_news_main.groupby(
    company_news_main['date'].dt.to_period('M')
)['vader_compound'].mean()
monthly_sent.index = monthly_sent.index.astype(str)
ax3.plot(monthly_sent.index, monthly_sent.values, marker='o', color='#1976d2',
         linewidth=2, markersize=8)
ax3.fill_between(monthly_sent.index, monthly_sent.values, alpha=0.15, color='#1976d2')
ax3.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
ax3.set_xlabel('Month')
ax3.set_ylabel('Avg VADER Compound')
ax3.set_title('C. Monthly Headline Sentiment Trend (2025-2026)', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax3.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

# --- Panel 4: Sentiment vs Earnings Surprise ---
ax4 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1])

# Rebuild earnings_sentiment for this plot
sector_map = {
    'AAPL': 'Technology', 'MSFT': 'Technology', 'GOOGL': 'Technology',
    'META': 'Technology', 'NVDA': 'Technology',
    'JPM': 'Finance', 'GS': 'Finance', 'BAC': 'Finance',
    'JNJ': 'Healthcare', 'UNH': 'Healthcare',
    'AMZN': 'Consumer', 'WMT': 'Consumer', 'KO': 'Consumer',
    'CAT': 'Industrial', 'BA': 'Industrial'
}
sector_colors = {
    'Technology': '#ab47bc', 'Finance': '#42a5f5', 'Healthcare': '#ef5350',
    'Consumer': '#66bb6a', 'Industrial': '#ffa726'
}

company_sent = company_news.groupby('symbol')['vader_compound'].mean().reset_index()
company_sent.columns = ['symbol', 'vader_mean']

surprise_col = None
for col in ['surprisePercent', 'surprise_pct', 'surprisepercentage']:
    if col in earnings_surprises.columns:
        surprise_col = col
        break
if surprise_col is None:
    earnings_surprises['surprise_pct'] = (
        (earnings_surprises['actual'] - earnings_surprises['estimate'])
        / earnings_surprises['estimate'].abs() * 100
    )
    surprise_col = 'surprise_pct'

company_earn = earnings_surprises.groupby('symbol')[surprise_col].mean().reset_index()
company_earn.columns = ['symbol', 'avg_surprise']

plot_df = company_sent.merge(company_earn, on='symbol')
plot_df['sector'] = plot_df['symbol'].map(sector_map)

for sector in plot_df['sector'].unique():
    subset = plot_df[plot_df['sector'] == sector]
    ax4.scatter(subset['vader_mean'], subset['avg_surprise'], s=120,
                color=sector_colors[sector], edgecolors='black', linewidth=0.5,
                label=sector, zorder=5)

for _, row in plot_df.iterrows():
    ax4.annotate(row['symbol'], (row['vader_mean'], row['avg_surprise']),
                 fontsize=8, ha='left', va='bottom')

ax4.set_xlabel('Average VADER Sentiment')
ax4.set_ylabel('Average Earnings Surprise %')
ax4.set_title('D. News Sentiment vs Earnings Surprise', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax4.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
ax4.legend(fontsize=9, loc='lower right')

plt.suptitle('Finnhub Financial News Analysis: Executive Summary',
             fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('executive_dashboard.png', dpi=200, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  6.2  TOPIC-SECTOR HEATMAP (POLISHED)
###############################################################################

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

topic_by_sector = company_news.groupby('sector')['top_topic'].value_counts(
    normalize=True
).unstack(fill_value=0).round(3)

topic_labels_short = [
    'T0: Corporate\n/Retail',
    'T1: Political\n/Trade',
    'T2: Market\n/Trading',
    'T3: Banking\n/Investment',
    'T4: Big Tech\n/AI'
]

sns.heatmap(
    topic_by_sector, annot=True, fmt='.1%', cmap='YlOrRd',
    xticklabels=topic_labels_short, ax=ax,
    cbar_kws={'label': 'Proportion of Articles'}
)
ax.set_title('Topic Distribution Across Industry Sectors', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('Sector')
ax.set_xlabel('')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('topic_sector_heatmap.png', dpi=200, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  6.3  TOPIC TRENDS WITH OLS LINES (POLISHED)
###############################################################################

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 7))

topic_labels_full = [
    'T0: Corporate/Retail', 'T1: Political/Trade', 'T2: Market/Trading',
    'T3: Banking/Investment', 'T4: Big Tech/AI'
]

company_news_main['month_num'] = (
    company_news_main['date'].dt.year * 12 + company_news_main['date'].dt.month
)
min_month = company_news_main['month_num'].min()
company_news_main['month_index'] = company_news_main['month_num'] - min_month

for i in range(5):
    monthly_topic = company_news_main.groupby('month_index')[f'topic_{i+1}'].mean()
    ax.scatter(monthly_topic.index, monthly_topic.values, s=40,
               color=topic_colors[i], alpha=0.7)
    # OLS trend line
    z = np.polyfit(monthly_topic.index, monthly_topic.values, 1)
    p = np.poly1d(z)
    x_line = np.linspace(monthly_topic.index.min(), monthly_topic.index.max(), 100)
    direction = "↑" if z[0] > 0 else "↓"
    ax.plot(x_line, p(x_line), color=topic_colors[i], linewidth=2.5,
            label=f'{topic_labels_full[i]} {direction}')

ax.set_xlabel('Month Index (0 = March 2025)')
ax.set_ylabel('Average Topic Probability')
ax.set_title('Topic Probability Trends Over Time (with OLS Fit)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(loc='best', fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('topic_trends_ols.png', dpi=200, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  6.4  CORRELATION HIGHLIGHT CHART
###############################################################################

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

# Key correlations with monthly return from Part 5
# Using the values from the correlation matrix
key_corrs = {
    'Topic 4\n(Banking/Invest)': 0.90,
    'Topic 2\n(Earnings/Trading)': 0.84,
    '% Positive\nHeadlines': 0.82,
    'VADER\nSentiment': 0.51,
    'Volatility': -0.51,
    'Topic 5\n(Big Tech/AI)': -0.67,
    'Topic 1\n(Political/Trade)': -0.67,
    'Topic 3\n(Market/Wall St)': -0.83
}

labels = list(key_corrs.keys())
values = list(key_corrs.values())
colors = ['#388e3c' if v > 0 else '#d32f2f' for v in values]

bars = ax.barh(labels, values, color=colors, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel('Correlation with Monthly Stock Return')
ax.set_title('Key Correlations: News Features vs Stock Returns', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.axvline(x=0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlim(-1, 1)

# Add value labels
for bar, val in zip(bars, values):
    ax.text(val + (0.03 if val > 0 else -0.03), bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f'{val:.2f}', va='center', ha='left' if val > 0 else 'right',
            fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('correlation_highlights.png', dpi=200, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

###############################################################################
#  6.5  PRINT FINAL CONCLUSIONS
###############################################################################

print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("  FINNHUB FINANCIAL NEWS ANALYSIS — FINAL CONCLUSIONS")
print("=" * 70)

print("""
PROJECT OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Analyzed 40,112 news articles from Finnhub API covering 15 major companies
across 5 sectors (Technology, Finance, Healthcare, Consumer, Industrial)
over a 12-month period (March 2025 – February 2026).

Applied NLP techniques: VADER sentiment analysis, TextBlob polarity,
LDA topic modeling, TF-IDF analysis, and OLS regression to examine
relationships between news content and market outcomes.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY FINDING 1: SENTIMENT LANDSCAPE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Overall headline sentiment skews slightly positive (mean VADER: 0.105)
• 42.6% neutral, 39.9% positive, 17.4% negative
• Amazon has the most positive coverage (0.146), Apple the least (0.073)
• Consumer sector leads in sentiment (0.125), Finance trails (0.093)
• VADER and TextBlob show moderate agreement (r = 0.37), confirming that
  context-aware scoring (VADER) captures nuances that word-level tools miss

KEY FINDING 2: TOPIC STRUCTURE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• LDA identified 5 distinct topics that map cleanly to business domains:
    T0: Corporate/Retail (johnson, walmart, amazon) — 15.5%
    T1: Political/Trade (trump, boeing, tariff)    — 16.5%
    T2: Market/Trading (earnings, dow, wall st)    — 23.2%
    T3: Banking/Investment (dividend, bank, fund)  — 24.8%
    T4: Big Tech/AI (nvidia, meta, apple)          — 20.1%
• Topics align with sectors: Boeing → 56% Topic 1, NVDA → 48% Topic 4,
  Goldman Sachs → 36% Topic 3
• Topic trends over time are statistically significant (p < 0.001):
    - Earnings/Market news (T2) increasing over the year
    - Political/Trade news (T1) decreasing
    - Big Tech/AI (T4) increasing (AI narrative growth)

KEY FINDING 3: MARKET IMPACT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Strong correlations between news features and stock returns:
    - Banking/Investment topic probability → returns (r = 0.90)
    - Earnings/Trading topic → returns (r = 0.84)
    - % positive headlines → returns (r = 0.82)
    - Political/Trade topic → returns (r = -0.67)
• Higher volatility negatively correlated with returns (r = -0.51)
• Simple VADER → returns OLS shows positive coefficient (0.045) but
  limited statistical power due to small monthly sample size

KEY FINDING 4: EARNINGS SURPRISE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Boeing is the major outlier: lowest sentiment + worst earnings surprise (-38%)
• Amazon shows highest sentiment + highest surprise (14.6%)
• Pre-earnings 30-day sentiment window shows weak predictive power (r = -0.09)
  suggesting news sentiment is reactive rather than predictive of earnings
• Most companies beat estimates (positive surprise), consistent with the known
  "lowball estimate" phenomenon in analyst forecasts

KEY FINDING 5: TF-IDF INSIGHTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• TF-IDF correctly identifies sector-specific vocabulary:
    - Technology: chip, gpu, ai, intelligence, cloud
    - Finance: dividend, loan, banking, portfolio
    - Healthcare: drug, patient, fda, clinical
    - Consumer: sugar, beverage, retail, delivery
    - Industrial: aircraft, defense, construction

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LIMITATIONS & FUTURE WORK
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Monthly aggregation reduces sample size for OLS (only ~12 months)
  → Weekly aggregation could improve statistical power
• VADER/TextBlob are general-purpose tools; finance-specific models
  like FinBERT could improve sentiment accuracy
• Finnhub free tier limits prevented access to:
  - Earnings call transcripts (premium endpoint)
  - News sentiment scores (pre-computed by Finnhub)
  - Insider sentiment data (mostly empty)
• Causality cannot be established — correlations could reflect
  reverse causation (good returns → positive news coverage)
• Topic model with 5 topics is one choice; coherence optimization
  could identify the optimal number

TOOLS & TECHNIQUES USED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Data Collection: Finnhub API (v4 pipeline)
• Text Preprocessing: regex, NLTK stopwords, WordNet lemmatization
• Sentiment Analysis: VADER, TextBlob
• Topic Modeling: LDA (lda package + CountVectorizer)
• Feature Analysis: TF-IDF (sklearn TfidfVectorizer)
• Statistical Modeling: OLS regression, Mixed Effects Model (statsmodels)
• Visualization: matplotlib, seaborn
""")

print("=" * 70)
print("  PROJECT COMPLETE")
print("=" * 70)
print(f"""
  Total Articles Analyzed:  {len(company_news):,}
  Companies:                15
  Sectors:                  5
  Date Range:               March 2025 – February 2026
  Topics Identified:        5
  Sentiment Models:         2 (VADER, TextBlob)
  OLS Regressions:          6+ models
  Visualizations:           20+ charts
""")

```
